texts.full_text: is selective death of motor neurons is still unclear, but among several pathomechanisms that have been discussed, the loss of neurotrophic factors is one hypothesis. Basic fibroblast gr...owth factor (bFGF) has emerged as a potential biomarker and therapeutic target in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS). bFGF, also known as FGF2, plays crucial roles in neuronal survival, differentiation, and regeneration. Studies have shown that the levels of bFGF are altered both in cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) and serum samples from sporadic ALS patients compared to healthy controls or other neurological disease groups such as Alzheimer's disease or multiple sclerosis. In CSF, some studies have reported decreased bFGF levels in sALS patients relative to control subjects (Liu et al., 2014), while others found no significant difference between the two cohorts but noted a correlation with clinical progression and disease severity scores such as ALS Functional Rating Scale-Revised or King's stage scale (Kimura et al., 2008). In serum, bFGF levels have been reported to be elevated in sALS patients compared to healthy controls. This increase might reflect a compensatory response by the body attempting to counteract neurodegeneration and promote neuronal survival
